coredata.dc:title: Dual functional carbon dots derived from cornflour via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Zhang, Xin
Sheng, Yingzhuo
Shen, Jianmin
Huang, Peng
Guo, Shikuan
Pan, Jiaqi
Feng, Boxue
coredata.dc:description: Highly Photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) with a PL quantum yield (PLQY) of 7.7% have been prepared from cornflour by a simple hydrothermal method. The as-prepared carbon dots have good water solubility, well distributed particle size, amorphous structure and acceptable fluorescence lifetime. Besides, due to the fairly good biocompatibility and outstanding fluorescent properties, the CDs were successfully applied to cell imaging and Cu2+ detecting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Luminescence
Biomaterials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and component characterization of CDs: (a) TEM image, inset: size distribution (n=100); (b) HRTEM images, inset: SAED pattern; (c) FTIR spectrum; (d) XPS, (e) C1s and (f) N1s spectra.

(a) Abs, PL emission of CDs, inset: optical images under daylight (left) and UV light (right); (b) PL emission spectra of CDs with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500nm; (c) normalized PL emission spectra; (d) PL decay curve of CDs.
(a) Cell viability by MTT assay, S180 sarcoma cells at the excitation wavelength of (b) 364 and (c) 488nm.

(a) PL spectra of CDs in the absence-(A) and presence-(B) of Cu2+, inset: the corresponding optical image under UV light; (b) PL spectra of CDs dispersion in the presence of different Cu2+ concentrations; (c) the dependence of F 0/F on the concentrations of Cu2+ ions; (d) the dependence of F 0/F with the blank and solutions containing different metal ions.

head.ce:title: Dual functional carbon dots derived from cornflour via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Plasma and Metal Materials, School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Plasma and Metal Materials, School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, School of Life Sciences, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:country: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, School of Life Sciences, Lanzhou UniversityChina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly Photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) with a PL quantum yield (PLQY) of 7.7% have been prepared from cornflour by a simple hydrothermal method. The as-prepared carbon dots have good water solubility, well distributed particle size, amorphous structure and acceptable fluorescence lifetime. Besides, due to the fairly good biocompatibility and outstanding fluorescent properties, the CDs were successfully applied to cell imaging and Cu2+ detecting.
Dual functional carbon dots derived from cornflour via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent CDs have attracted growing interest in recent years due to their great potentials in biological labeling, bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic device applications [1–3], and CDs are emerging as viable alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) owing to their important photoluminescent properties and lack of any known cytotoxicity [4]. These advantages have led to increased interests in developing methods for their syntheses, involving both top-down and bottom-up approaches [5]. Several top-down approaches such as laser ablation have been proposed to produce fluorescent CDs [6]. On the other hand, bottom-up approaches like carbonization of glucose, sucrose, glycol, citric acids, etc. have achieved signiﬁcant attention for the production of fluorescent CDs [7]. However, these approaches commonly suffer from the involvement of complex or post-treatment processes, severe synthetic conditions, and the use of large amounts of strong acid or expensive starting materials, which severely limit the practical applications of CDs. In this paper, we report that the water-soluble fluorescent CDs can be readily prepared by a simple, one-step, green hydrothermal method using low-cost, natural corn as carbon source. The fairly good biocompatibility and unique luminescence properties can enable the as-prepared CDs to be applied to cell imaging and Cu2+ detecting.

Cornflour was purchased from the local flourmill and was used without further purification. The CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of cornflour in deionized (DI) water. Briefly, 0.5g of cornflour was dispersed into 30mL DI water under magnetic stirring and sonicated for 30min. Then the mixture was transferred into a 40mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 5h. After the completion of the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. A dark brown solution was obtained; the solution was ﬁltrated to separate the deposit, and the brown filtrate was then centrifuged at high speed to obtain highly fluorescent CDs.

The obtained CDs exhibit fairly good monodispersity, a uniform spherical morphology ( Fig. 1(a)), and the diameters of CDs are in the range of 2–6nm, with an average value of 3.5nm (inset of Fig. 1(a)). Fig. 1(b) shows the amorphous structure of the CDs, which also can be confirmed by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern. As shown in Fig. 1(c), these characteristic absorption bands of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum indicate the existence of the functional groups including carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy groups and amino group [8,9]. The presence of these functional groups imparts excellent solubility of CDs in water without further chemical modiﬁcation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. 1(d)) was taken to further investigate the surface state and component of the CDs, and the results revealed that the CDs contained carbon, oxygen and nitrogen, which may come from the amylum and protein in the cornflour, and the calculated molar ratio of C, O and N is C:O:N=11:2.3:1. In detail, the C1s and N1s spectra (Fig. 1(e) and (f)) demonstrate the presence of C–C, C–N and C–O.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), a strong absorption peak can be seen at 282nm, which is similar with the previous report [10]. The CDs solution shows a strong PL emission peak centered at 441nm when CDs were excited at 360nm, indicating the CDs are fluorescent. The CDs aqueous solution is canary yellow and transparent in daylight but changes to bright blue under UV excitation (λ ex= 365nm) (inset of Fig. 2(a)). The detailed PL investigation was carried out with different excitation wavelengths, Fig. 2(b)–(c) show the typical excitation-dependent PL behavior of CDs [11]. The PLQY of the obtained CDs is 7.7%, which was calculated using quinine sulfate as the standard [12]. Fig. 2(d) shows the PL decay curve and the instrument response function; the observed lifetimes of CDs are τ 1=0.74ns, τ 2=2.88ns, and τ 3=9.69ns. The lifetime in the magnitude of nanosecond suggests that the synthesized CDs are most suitable for biological application [13].

Fig. 3(a) shows the viabilities of L02 hepatocyte and S180 sarcoma cell incubated with different concentrations of CDs. The high viabilities indicate that the as-prepared CDs can be considered to be safe for in vitro and in vivo applications. Due to the excellent water-solubility, tunable luminescence and high biocompatibility, the obtained CDs promise potential applications in biolabelling. As shown in Fig. 3(b) and (c), the bright luminescence of CDs in S180 sarcoma cells at different excitations was observed by laser scanning confocal microscope. These observations demonstrate that the obtained CDs can serve as an ideal probe for cell imaging.

It is well known that the determination of copper in the environment and organisms is very significant [14]. Firstly, we explored the feasibility of using the as-prepared CDs for Cu2+ detection in Phosphate Buffered Solution (PBS, pH=7.0). Fig. 4(a) shows the PL emission spectra of the CDs aqueous solution in the absence and presence Cu2+. The presence of Cu2+ lead to an obvious decrease of fluorescence in intensity, indicating that the Cu2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs, which can be attributed to the chelation of Cu2+ with N and O and lead to substantial fluorescence quenching [15]. In addition, the PL emission spectra of CDs solutions with different concentrations of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to100μM (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 100μM) were measured for the sensitivity study (Fig. 4(b)), showing that the PL intensity of the mixture is sensitive to Cu2+ concentration and decreases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. Fig. 4(c) shows F 0/F on different concentrations of Cu2+, where the F 0 and F are CDs fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. Based on the results, the estimated detection limit (1nM) is comparable to that of CDs previously reported [15–17], and is lower than that of nanodots based on heavy metal ions such as CdS QDs [18]. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the CDs, the PL intensity of CDs aqueous solutions with different metal ions under the same conditions were investigated (Fig. 4(d)). It can be found that a much lower PL was observed for CDs upon addition of Cu2+, and slight decrease was observed by addition of other metal ions into the CDs solutions. Moreover, when Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+ or Hg2+ ions exists in the detecting system as interfering ions, these metal ions only have slight influence on the fluorescence of the system. These observations reveal the high selectivity of the obtained CDs for Cu2+.

In conclusion, water soluble fluorescent CDs were successfully prepared via a simple, green hydrothermal method from natural precursor. The obtained CDs show good monodispersity with 3.5nm average particle size and tunable luminescence which is dependent on the excitation wavelength. The biocompatibility of CDs was evaluated, indicating the extremely low cytotoxicity. Furthermore, due to the above advantages the as-prepared CDs can be used as cell imaging probe and fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Grant nos. 61006001 and 61176005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2014.02.090.
